586: 	double (* __restrict u)[n2][n1] = (double (*)[n2][n1])pointer_u;


[...]


600: 			for(i2 = 0; i2 < mm2-1; i2++){


601:                 // Vectorizing intermediate data preparation


602:                 #pragma omp simd


603: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1; i1++){


604: 					z1[i1] = z[i3][i2+1][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


605: 					z2[i1] = z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


606: 					z3[i1] = z[i3+1][i2+1][i1] + z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z1[i1];


607: 				}


608:                 // Optimization: Forced SIMD to mitigate strided access overhead (2*i1)


609:                 #pragma omp simd


610: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


611: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1] += z[i3][i2][i1];


612: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1+1] += 0.5*(z[i3][i2][i1+1]+z[i3][i2][i1]);


613: 				}


614:                 #pragma omp simd


615: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


616: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1] += 0.5 * z1[i1];


617: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] += 0.25*( z1[i1] + z1[i1+1] );


618: 				}


619:                 #pragma omp simd


620: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


621: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1] += 0.5 * z2[i1];


622: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1+1] += 0.25*( z2[i1] + z2[i1+1] );


623: 				}


624:                 #pragma omp simd


625: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


626: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1] += 0.25* z3[i1];


627: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] += 0.125*( z3[i1] + z3[i1+1] );

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 107.33 cycles (1.47x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 58% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 126.95 cycles (1.24x speedup).

Details


66% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 64% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 68% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 57% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 92% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 217 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 157.67 cycles. At this rate:

  • 43% of peak load performance is reached (41.96 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 27% of peak store performance is reached (17.61 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 124.67 cycles (1.26x speedup).

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 107.33 cycles (1.47x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 58% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 126.95 cycles (1.24x speedup).

Details


66% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 64% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 68% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 57% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 92% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 217 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 157.67 cycles. At this rate:

  • 43% of peak load performance is reached (41.96 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 27% of peak store performance is reached (17.61 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 157.67 to 124.67 cycles (1.26x speedup).
